turns.content: I'm confused about the difference between using a &str slice and an owned String when I need to handle legacy Latin-1 or Windows-1252 data coming from an old CSV file.

The main distinction is that &str is just a reference with no ownership, so if your source data contains non-UTF-8 bytes like the 0x80 character common in Latin-1, you cannot store it in a &str or a String directly. You must use the encoding_rs crate to parse those raw bytes into a String, which internally uses UTF-8 characters and can represent that same 0x80 byte as the character '€'.

That clarifies ownership, but I'm still stuck on how exactly does encoding_rs detect if the incoming data is actually Latin-1 versus pure UTF-8? Does it just assume everything is one thing or do I have to write custom logic to check for that BOM header first?

You don't need custom logic to check for a BOM; you should always read the bytes into a Vec<u8> first and then use encoding_rs::from_utf8() on them. That function will return an Option<Result<String>>. If there is a UTF-8 BOM (0xEF 0xBB 0xBF), it returns Ok(String) with the BOM removed from the output string. If the data is pure Latin-1, that same call returns Err(Latin1Error::InvalidUtf8), which you can catch and then convert using encoding_rs::from_utf8_latin1() instead.
